---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, workshop_data_availability.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    footer: "DSPG 2026 | Virginia Tech"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true
    incremental: false
---

## {.title-custom data-menu-title="Data Availability Workshop"}

::: {.title-block}
::: {.t-title}
Organizing and Documenting Data Availability
:::
::: {.t-rule}
:::
::: {.t-authors}
**Instructor:** Pragati Dahal
:::
::: {.t-program}
Data Science for the Public Good · Summer 2026
:::
::: {.t-logos}
![](images/kohlcentre_logo.png){height="70"}
:::
:::

---

## What Are We Building Today?

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:
:::
- **Identify** which variables exist in your dataset across years
- **Audit** data availability programmatically in R
- **Document** your findings in a structured, shareable format
- **Visualize** the results as an interactive HTML dashboard
:::

::: {.note}
Before we start — open the finished dashboard in your browser. That is where we are going.
:::

---

## Where This Fits in Your Project

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
The problem
:::
Before you clean your data, you need to know what you actually have.
If a variable only exists for 7 out of 14 years — you need to know that *before* you spend hours cleaning it.
:::

::: {.scope-conditional}
::: {.mini-heading}
Today's step
:::
**Raw Data → [YOU ARE HERE] → Cleaning → Analysis → Report**

The step between downloading data and cleaning it is usually skipped. Today we make it explicit.
:::

---

## tidycensus: Your Data Access Tool

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
You set up your Census API key in the previous workshop. Now we use it.
:::
`tidycensus` gives you ACS data directly in R — no manual downloading required.
:::

```r
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)

get_acs(
  geography = "county",      # unit of analysis
  variables = "B19013_001",  # which variable
  state     = "VA",          # which state
  year      = 2022,          # which year
  survey    = "acs5"         # 5-year estimates
)
```

---

## Finding Variable Codes

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
ACS variables follow a pattern: Table + Cell number
:::
`B19013_001` = Table B19013 (Median Household Income), Cell 001

Use `load_variables()` to search the full list:
:::

```r
vars <- load_variables(2022, "acs5", cache = TRUE)
View(vars)

# Search by keyword
vars |>
  filter(str_detect(label, "income|Income")) |>
  select(name, label, concept)
```

::: {.note}
Always check the `concept` column — it tells you which table the variable belongs to.
:::

---

## Your First Data Pull

```r
va_income <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B19013_001",
  state     = "VA",
  year      = 2022,
  survey    = "acs5"
)

head(va_income)
```

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
Output
:::
Each row is one county. Five columns: `GEOID`, `NAME`, `variable`, `estimate`, `moe`

- **estimate** = the ACS value
- **moe** = margin of error
:::

---

## Pulling Multiple Years

::: {.note}
`map_dfr()` means: run this function for each year, then stack all results into one dataframe.
:::

```r
years <- 2015:2022

multi_year <- map_dfr(years, function(yr) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "county",
    variables = c(
      median_income = "B19013_001",
      poverty       = "B17001_002"
    ),
    state  = "VA",
    year   = yr,
    survey = "acs5"
  ) |>
    mutate(year = yr)
})
```

---

## The Problem: Not All Variables Exist

```r
get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B28002_004",  # broadband access
  state     = "VA",
  year      = 2012,
  survey    = "acs5"
)
```

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
You get an error
:::
ACS did not track broadband subscriptions at the county level before 2017.

This is not a data error — it is measurement history. And you need to know this *before* you build your analysis panel.
:::

---

## The Audit Function

::: {.note}
`tryCatch()` lets R handle errors gracefully — instead of crashing, it returns `FALSE`.
:::

```r
check_variable <- function(var, year) {
  tryCatch({
    result <- get_acs(
      geography = "county",
      variables = var,
      state     = "VA",
      year      = year,
      survey    = "acs5"
    )
    nrow(result) > 0     # TRUE if data exists
  }, error = function(e) {
    FALSE                # FALSE if error
  })
}

check_variable("B28002_004", 2012)   # FALSE
check_variable("B28002_004", 2020)   # TRUE
```

---

## Running the Full Audit

```r
vars <- c(
  "B19013_001",  "B17001_002",  "B15003_022",
  "B27001_001",  "B01003_001",  "B28002_004"
)

years <- 2010:2023

audit_results <- expand_grid(variable = vars, year = years) |>
  mutate(available = map2_lgl(variable, year, check_variable))

write_csv(audit_results, "audit_results.csv")
```

::: {.note}
This makes ~84 API calls and takes a few minutes. Save immediately with `write_csv()` so you never have to run it again.
:::

---

## What the Audit Reveals

| Variable | Coverage | Missing Years |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| Total Population | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Median Household Income | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Population Below Poverty | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Bachelor's Degree | ⚠️ 12/14 | 2010–2011 |
| Health Insurance Universe | ⚠️ 12/14 | 2010–2011 |
| **Broadband Access** | **⚠️ 7/14** | **2010–2016** |

::: {.note}
If your project needs broadband data, your analysis window starts in 2017 — not 2010. This is the kind of thing the audit is designed to surface.
:::

---

## Adding Labels and Categories

```r
var_metadata <- tibble(
  variable = c("B19013_001","B17001_002","B15003_022",
               "B27001_001","B01003_001","B28002_004"),
  label    = c("Median Household Income","Population Below Poverty",
               "Bachelor's Degree","Health Insurance Universe",
               "Total Population","Broadband Access"),
  category = c("Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic",
               "Healthcare Access","Demographics","Infrastructure")
)

audit_final <- audit_results |>
  left_join(var_metadata, by = "variable") |>
  group_by(variable, label, category) |>
  summarise(
    n_years       = sum(available),
    years_present = list(year[available == TRUE]),
    missing_years = list(year[available == FALSE]),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

---

## Building the Dashboard

::: {.contrib-grid}
::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 1 — Pure R
:::
```r
library(reactable)
library(htmlwidgets)

widget <- reactable(
  audit_final,
  filterable  = TRUE,
  searchable  = TRUE
)

saveWidget(widget,
  "data_availability.html")
```
Functional, shareable, no AI needed.
:::

::: {.contrib-column}
::: {.contrib-heading}
Option 2 — R + Claude
:::
```r
library(jsonlite)

cat(toJSON(audit_final |>
  mutate(
    years_present =
      map(years_present, as.integer),
    missing_years =
      map(missing_years, as.integer)
  ), pretty = TRUE))
```
Copy JSON → paste into Claude → download polished HTML dashboard.
:::
:::

---

## Now You Try {background-color="#861F41"}

::: {.workflow}
::: {.workflow-step}
::: {.step-number}
1
:::
::: {.card-title}
Open
:::
Open `workshop_exercise.R` from the shared folder
:::
::: {.workflow-step}
::: {.step-number}
2
:::
::: {.card-title}
Load
:::
`acs_virginia_raw.csv` is pre-loaded — no API calls needed
:::
::: {.workflow-step}
::: {.step-number}
3
:::
::: {.card-title}
Fill
:::
Fill in the **3 gaps** marked `# YOUR CODE HERE`
:::
::: {.workflow-step}
::: {.step-number}
4
:::
::: {.card-title}
Build
:::
Run the audit and generate your dashboard
:::
::: {.workflow-step}
::: {.step-number}
5
:::
::: {.card-title}
Extend
:::
If you finish early — try Option 2 with Claude
:::
:::

::: {.note}
Solution is in `workshop_solution.R` if you get stuck.
:::

---

## Apply This to Your Own Project

::: {.question-grid}
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 1 — Read your proposal
:::
What variables do you need? Write them down before touching any code.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 2 — Find the codes
:::
Use `load_variables()` to find the ACS table and cell for each variable you need.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 3 — Run the audit
:::
Which years are actually available? Don't assume — check programmatically.
:::
::: {.question-card}
::: {.question-title}
Step 4 — Document
:::
Add your own labels and categories. Save the audit script — it is part of your project documentation.
:::
:::

::: {.note}
Rural Health & AVI teams: broadband analysis window starts in 2017, not 2010.
:::

---

## Summary

::: {.scope-core}
::: {.mini-heading}
What you learned today
:::
- How to pull ACS data by variable code using `tidycensus`
- How to audit variable availability across years with `tryCatch()`
- Why some variables have gaps — and why that matters for your analysis
- How to document findings with human-readable labels and categories
- How to turn your audit into a shareable HTML dashboard
:::

::: {.scope-conditional}
::: {.mini-heading}
The pipeline
:::
`get_acs()` → `check_variable()` → `expand_grid()` → `reactable` / Claude → HTML
:::

---

## Questions? {.title-custom}

::: {.title-block}
::: {.t-title}
Thank you
:::
::: {.t-rule}
:::
::: {.t-authors}
**Pragati Dahal** <br>
Graduate Research Assistant · Agricultural & Applied Economics <br>
Virginia Tech <br>
pdahal23@vt.edu
:::
::: {.t-program}
Workshop materials available in the shared DSPG folder
:::
::: {.t-logos}
![](images/kohlcentre_logo.png){height="70"}
:::
:::
